entry.title: featherworks
pause
entry.content:
Tomorrow evening, the kids and I will be at John's grandmother's place, watching the sun set over the bay. This is probably the last summer we'll be able to spend time at the beach house: John's grandmother passed away in December and the house most likely will be sold at the end of the season.

Ten random traditions we'll be observing over the next ten days:

:: Morning coffee on the dock
:: Saying goodbye to the sun on the dock
:: Midnight visits to the dock (can you tell we like the dock?)
:: A large pie with onions, sweet peppers and mushrooms from Nicola Pizza in Rehoboth
:: Fisher's caramel corn in Bethany (can you tell we like to eat?)
:: Book browsing at Bethany Beach Books
:: Letterboxing at James Farm, which is within walking distance of the house via road or squishy bay bottom at low-tide
:: $1 toy shopping at Fun For All in Rehoboth
:: Clamming at low tide, followed by our famous clams casino (again with the eating)
:: Wading to the far shore of the cove to hunt for dessicated baby horseshoe crabs (not for eating)

I'll miss you while I'm away, but I promise to send you a postcard. Or a picture. Better yet, hop in the car and drive on over. We'll be waiting at the end of the dock.
Good one. Where, oh where, do you find textiles like that? Surely not in ye olde upstate NY?

entry.title: national zoo | washington, dc
entry.content:
Things I learned this day:

+ I can finally get my hair back into a ponytail, and that is where it shall stay for the hot Virginia summertime.

+ Metro escalator repair men will sometimes stop you to talk cameras and lenses at the Woodley Park exit.

+ Diplomatic motorcades are really, really loud when they drive by and they go stupid fast, which you would think would defeat the purpose of not drawing unwelcome attention to the occupants of the vehicles. But they're the pros and I am just a mom.

+ When one elephant pees, the others in its enclosure tend to follow suit. The trainers call it a "pee party". Now I can check elephant pee party off of my list of things to see before I die.

+ Riding the Metro as part of a posse of five kids and two moms with a stroller and a giant backpack is much more fun than riding the Metro as a single working woman wearing ear buds and clutching a briefcase.
Lovely. If you want heat, come on by. It's 80 degrees.

entry.title: i said yes
entry.content:
It was hard work. I had to lift heavy pine branches over my head to finish the lean-to, and while I held still to staple the sides of the tee pees, the mosquitoes nearly ate my face off. In fact, one bit me on the left side of my lower lip, and I have been doing dead-on Silvester Stallone impressions all afternoon. Adriaaaaaaan!

I wanted to tell them no, because I am old and tired and over-caffeinated. But this was the first weekend after the first week of a new school year, the end of the first week of our new days spent more apart than together. So I said yes instead. Yes, I will play with you. Yes, I like what you do. I am sore and beat and I got my new sneakers muddy. Isn't that great?

entry.title: the gift in death
entry.content:
It's very hard to write eloquently about a subject that's already been written into the ground. A death in the family tears you off of your feet and out of the everyday, unwilling, stunned into silence, glancing over your shoulder at what should have been as you begin walking the rut of the mourner. Gather at Mom's house, wash dishes, murmur reassurances of eternal life through Jesus that land on unwilling ears. Bake the casserole, wipe off the table, wince as you overhear every unfeeling cliché being directed at the widower. And as you work you pine away for the normal, not just for yourself but for the whole family. You'd rather Sunday never happened. If only we could be grocery shopping, solving math problems at the school table, putting in the next load of laundry. At this time of year we should be at Mom's house to decorate Christmas cookies. Instead we keep busy with glasses and hot plates and the napkin pile as Mom prepares to bury her youngest child.

And yet there is a gift: we are reminded that we are alive. We have today. We remember that we are here on Earth for a reason. At this moment my reason is to set out bagels for those who mourn, to hold people I love close to me, to put out the butter bells. But the next moment it may be to share the reason for the hope that I have. And in so doing my faith ignites and burns, my love for He who is in me is renewed, and I am once more brought to my knees, so grateful for a God who came to Earth as a baby to deliver us. Through death, we sometimes find life, again.
That night I was as graceful as an elk while trying to cook dinner, sidestepping threads and maneuvering hither and thither. Not.

entry.title: a world
entry.content:
I made a world for them. When I couldn't find the perfect bunny house for sale, I glued wood and moss and stone, late into the night. I crafted grass, and a pond, and a path between the two bunny families. I felted rugs and bowls, sewed tiny curtains and pillows. I released my love into each piece, working as they slept, and with every stitch I sent thoughts to my little ones:
I love you.
I believe in you.
I am so happy to be your mommy.

entry.title: sounds
entry.content: I water my garden every morning. When I'm finished, I sit on my front steps, tuck my hands between my knees for warmth, and listen. The water makes a crackling noise as it seeps into the concrete at my feet. If the windows of the passing cars are open, which they often are, I can make out the wax and wane of NPR or Coldplay or Taylor Swift, or an ad for a car dealership in the city. Through the open door behind me I hear the kids crunching their cereal in the dining room. They both sing wordless, keening little kid songs between bites. I close my eyes and memorize the sounds.

entry.title: family nest
entry.content:
We gave up a family bed when our youngest was about two and a half years old. By that time we had been sleeping together in one bed as a family since our oldest was first born, four years earlier. We loved our family bed, but each one of us was quite ready for some more sleeping space, our own comfortable area in which to stretch out and rest without being under direct threat of flailing limbs and pinched tresses. I didn't sleep a wink the first night we slept in our own big girl/boy beds, but both children did. And they woke up with smiles on their faces.

But sometimes, on chilly fall and winter nights, after a long day of living and loving together, we're not quite ready to go our separate ways at bedtime. Sometimes we make a family nest and sleep in it together like a colony of rabbits. In winter we build a big fire in the wood stove, drag a million pillows and blankets down the stairs, and make the fluffiest, warmest, most inviting space to cuddle. We play games, read books, play...and sleep. Yesterday was Daddy's birthday, so we celebrated in style in our first family nest of autumn. After a long and chilly night, I built a fire in the pre-dawn light and waited for the little ones to wake up.
Very nice. I'm still not going blonde, though.

*Chills* because "yes" is such an important word and yet such a stinkin' hard word (especially when it is cold outside. or blood sucking bugs are present. or you have new sneakers on). Is the thing to hold onto knowing they'll look back and remember that lean on? Or maybe, its getting to see their smiles and hear their joy right there and then-- especially during times of transition and new stuff. PS-- I almost peed in my pants of the arsenic hour. PPS- I almost peed my pants again because YOU commented over there, and I love your blog so darn much.

entry.title: husks
entry.content: We have many nests in our home. They are filled with rocks, shells, eggs, toys, and husks. The cicada husk reminds me of one hot summer in Virginia when my daughter was small and the cicadas flew thick and loud. I found the fuzzy bee body on the back deck one fall morning, but I haven't had the time to figure out its species.

entry.title: a wednesday
entry.content: A little one acting not quite right. A pizza lunch at the amphitheater, sheltered from the drizzle. A visit to the library. A confession: a sore throat since morning. A quick ride home. A fever. A bed.
I know I know I think we all feel a little guilty when we do, your time will come!

I'm either going to move in or make my living room cozier. I can't do anything about the lack of snow though. I wish that we had it all winter long, like we did in Canada and Chicago. I would love to go cross country skiing every day. Remember when I told you that we walked to the grocery store in the winter in Canada? Because they plowed well, streets and sidewalks...and when the drifts got too high, they'd load 'em up in dump trucks and dump the snow on the bay. Well, we used to pull my baby neice along in a little sled, all bundled up. Totally cute. Those were different days. No one around here wants to be pulled along in a sled anymore.
@linny do milkmen even exist anymore? @kate you slay me. and there is no such thing as too much information, we are women.

entry.title: her note
entry.content:
Six years ago today I sat next to my mother's hospital bed and held her hand, listening for her final breath. I watched the spiky glowing line of her heart monitor flatten into a solid neon smoothness. After she was gone, I loosened the neck of her hospital gown and gently stroked her still-warm belly, criss-crossed by the stretch marks I had made from within her.

When I was twelve years old, she wrote this letter to me, just before heading into the surgery that would define the rest of her life. She was not sure she would survive the operating table, and wanted to make certain that I knew just how much she loved me. I keep this note in my underwear drawer. I take it out and read it on days when I need her.

entry.title: late winter walk
entry.content: We walked. The rivulets running across the path were choked with layers of leaves, mud and twigs, and thick, dark clumps stuck to the sides of my boots. I hugged my camera to my belly and hopped across rocks through deeper, freely running waters. They ran ahead, but I lingered at the sides of the path. Brittle mushroom skins covered a tree with brown, curling scales. I stooped to feel moss and bark. I turned to admire an empty peanut shell in a hollow. A single red berry clung to the end of a dark red, thorny stem. I heard their voices calling to me from deep within the wood. I looked up, and found that I was alone.
I am always astounded by the power of sunsets and sunrises. Beautiful photo and words

entry.title: nightgown
entry.content: The babysitter is in the kitchen. I think she's sitting at the table, reading the paper and smoking a cigarette, but the door is closed so I am not sure. I am in the next room, the living room. I am smiling. I lovingly arrange my mother's pink polyester nightgown on my little rocking chair. It's her favorite nightgown and so when I am lonely for her I take it out of her dresser and I spend time with it. Its sleeves are slippery and so over and over I have to put them back on the arms of the rocking chair, but I don't mind. The babysitter comes into the room and tries to ask me questions about the nightgown but I don't speak to her. I am thinking only of my mother. I scoop the nightgown up in my arms and I'm giving her a hug. I push my face into it and I am cuddling her. I drag it around the room by an arm and I'm holding her hand. And we dance together to Kenny Rogers on the radio.
Ooooh! This sounds delicious! I see that I am a few days late though. Any leftovers?

entry.title: woods edge
entry.content:
A sign hangs at the beginning of the long driveway, but I only catch the first word, Woods, white painted letters on dark pine. We pass fenced pastures dotted with small barns and stands of tall trees, only tufts of orange and brown leaves left at their very tops, their bodies spare streaks below. The horses lift their heads as we pass. We park under an old maple and haul folding chairs, blankets, and backpacks holding flashlights and sweatshirts past the farm house and into the back yard where the gathering has started.

This is Angelina's farm. She is a former lawyer turned farm-owner. Her long, black hair hangs down her back, almost brushing her thick leather belt. Many of her family are here, and friends, and friends of friends like we are, standing around the food tables or sitting in small groups. Children run through and between the barns, jump into the leaf piles and then pull poking leaf bones from their hair and pockets. The sun hangs low. Thick rays skim the tops of the dry branches piled high in the fire pit. The air smells of horse and hay, salad dressing and vegetable stew, grass and sun-warmed wood.

We eat and laugh. The men pick up guitars and try out a tune or two. The grey mare gives rides to the little ones, circling in the pea gravel until the passing three wheeler spooks her to a standstill. The sky deepens and the barn darkens as the children take turns swinging blindly at a pinata hanging from a cross beam. One final crack of the broom handle and candy pours from the fatal wound onto the cement floor.

The moon is round and orange. The sweet mares whinny for attention and carrots from their fences. We sit near the fire and watch the bats make laps around the horse barn, passing in front of the floodlight with each go-around. We circle little limbs and pull blanket corners down and around body edges. A flashlight hunt for a lost belonging takes place in the giant leaf pile; we hear a shout of victory and know that what was once lost is now found. Sparks fly upward.
I would sit there too... beautiful.

entry.title: 49: a declaration
entry.content: When I am forty nine years old, I will still fit into my size two jeans. I will look hot in my reading glasses. I will know more about wine, history and Latin than I do now. I will be slow to speak and slow to anger. I will finally have stopped biting my nails. I will live in a smallish house built in a clearing in the woods somewhere where there are four seasons, and there will be cats and a big garden and lots of bird feeders everywhere. And I will drive a convertible with the top down and won't care about what it does to my hair.
Bet you don't have any bugs in that window.
Yeah, and they can name it Eat It, You'll Like It.
I'd just like to point out that I did not Photoshop out my permanently furrowed brow or the smile lines around my mouth. But I wanted to.

love this post.

AND. I registered with Apt Therapy to thumbs up your fantastic project. But the thumbs up icon is just chasing arrows and won't let me click. Any thoughts?

Sorrow at Christmas. It's unthinkable. Yet, once again, my dear friend, you have allowed the Holy Spirit to work through you to be a healing influence, (in the midst of overwhelming loss), to family, friends, and strangers alike. See what a blessing you are? I'm lifting you & John up before the Throne of Grace, that you may be held in the palm of His hand.

entry.title: thirty seven
entry.content: I turned 37 years old on an eastbound Metro train. Two German women sat talking across the aisle from me. I caught every tenth word or so. A Vietnam veteran and his family waved from the front of the car, thanking me again for guiding them through their passcard purchase back at the Vienna station. A warm and little six year-old hand rested on my belly skin for comfort. I closed my eyes and felt the curve of the tracks beneath me.
You wild woman, you! What exactly are they preaching over at that Bible church of yours? Apparently, I need to start going THERE!
gorgeous!
sharp photos. wish I was there!

entry.title: the bud and the withered
dinner party post mortem
entry.content: It was one of those parties where you talk to yourself in the bathroom mirror: you talk about how much fun you're having and then you look yourself in the eye and say that maybe you should slow down on the wine. You speak French much of the night, and are complemented on your accent by your expat hosts. You catch your husband's eye from across the table and with one raised eyebrow you broadcast plans for later. That kind of party. You adjust your scarf, check your teeth, and then head back into the fray.

entry.title: it's always ourselves we find in the sea
entry.content: When we are here there is no hurrying and no herding and no lunch-packing, no prying small hands off of my body, no pushes toward the school door, no blue eyes full of longing to be home. We walk along the bay bottom at low tide and dig, and we poke at slimy things with long sticks. We pick up the hermit crabs, and we talk about how we love our visits to the beach, and we don't talk about the times in between. We are different people when we are here.

I myself am a spatial-sequence synesthete...I have seen number lines, days of the week, and months of the year in my head (in rotating 3-D)for as long as I can remember. I too assumed that everyone saw things like I did. I was in high-school before I realized other people didn't see numbers in their head. I didn't try to hide it, I just didn't think it was unusual! I see it as a great gift and my 9-year-old has it as well. Have you talked with other members of your family to see who else has a form of Synesthesia? My dad and brother also have forms of it. We have had so much fun sharing with each other!

entry.title: staying home
entry.content: Beginning last summer, John and I felt a need to be really present at home and in our neighborhood. We planted a garden and hung out more with neighbors. We listened to the church bells ring through our little village and tucked into the moment. Now in the deep winter the pull of the local dovetails nicely with my cyclical, borderline agoraphobic, hermit-like desire to stay inside my house until spring. I was pleasantly surprised to read this article and discover that I am accidentally hip, timely and eco-conscious, it seems.
Lovely indeed :-)

I am so sorry for your family's loss. Your post had special meaning for me. Tonight I am staying up late trying to exhaust myself so that when I do go to bed I will hopefully sleep. Tomorrow morning my mom will get the results of her PET scan so she will know if she is still cancer free. I am sitting here trying to keep my mind busy. Then, I came across your blog and your words brought me to tears. Life is so fragile and so short and so precious. I feel like just recently the reality of how short life is has hit me and I struggle with what my purpose is in life. What you said makes me realize that maybe we don't have to have some big, dramatic, flamboyant purpose. I suppose our love of God and all the little things in life like setting out bagels are enough. Take care and know that your family is in my prayers. Jill

entry.title: the heckled hen
daughter love
entry.content:
Last week we sat together on a single chair at Starbucks, waiting for our Frappucino. I piled her long limbs into my lap and circled her with my arms, holding in her angles and big feet. I rubbed my nose into her hair, and she closed her eyes. "You two are so in love," said a woman to us in passing. "That's so rare to see."

She lingered in the kitchen with me all of this morning while I made scones and coffee. She slipped out for a few minutes, then returned and handed me this card. I whispered my thanks into her sweet, soft neck.

(linking up to emily today...)

That's great that you said yes and played with them...so many times we let life get in the way of just enjoying our children, they grow to quickly and soon they won't be asking us to play or make tee pees, so you go girl! Christina B.

entry.title: a head full of fluff
entry.content:
My part of the world is being hit hard by the Orange Death. Thick deposits of Tang-colored powder cover my car, the grill, the white patio table and chairs I picked from the neighbor's trash freecycled last week in another spasm of accidental eco-hipness. My throat is raw and my blood is most likely 50% pollen at this point, so I've been staying inside quite a bit lately, pretending to be a writer.

I've been coordinating with editors on some submissions that are already on retainer. I've been sending queries, which makes me feel like a trollop begging for fifteen minutes in the backseat. But mostly I've been starting things. I've started essays, letters, chapters, projects, collaborations, stuff that could be good if I'd only focus on it. The starting is easy, but the finishing is dreadfully slow, and I am a procrastinator from waaaaay back. In fact, right after typing that last sentence, I wasted five minutes in front of the window, watching the helicopters fall from the maples and thinking that later today my little ones will drop handfuls of them from the second floor deck to watch them twirl to the ground. Then I stared at my cuffs and wished my favorite khakis from Old Navy weren't falling apart quite yet, and began devising a scheme to patch them with an old bed sheet I thrifted from the Salvation Army. Then I stared at the used coffee grounds for a while and thought about how much they look like Polaroid SX-70 manipulations I've seen around flickr lately.

People, I am in trouble. Simply sitting back down at the computer does not end the procrastination insanity. The Internet is like a giant window that looks out on a giant stand of maple trees that are all shedding their helicopters at once. My hope is that if I pass on these distractions to you, I'll be free of them forever and might be able to put two sentences together today. So here goes, a (partial) list of stuff I am looking at instead of working, but don't say I didn't warn you:

+ Analogue Daydreams, a documentary-style amateur video explaining the art of Holga photography

+ A video interview with Karen Laborie, illustrator and friend. I spent two summers at her home in southeast France when I was a teenager, and now she's gone and growed up on me. The interview is in French, so you might want to warm up your brain a little before watching. But isn't she CUTE??

+ Brian Ferry (no, not of Roxy Music) takes the best pictures and makes some great mixes on 8tracks, too

+ My kids and I have been laughing for two days over all the franglais on the McDonald's France website. "Le cheeseburger" hits their funny bone especially hard for some reason, maybe it's because of the thick French accent (and goofy face) I use when I pronounce it for them

+ I've been half-heartedly monitoring the mass-hysteria resulting from ABC's story on unschooling. I'm amused by the hoopla and grateful for having dodged that particular educational bullet

+ Listening to WQJZ's live webcast, complete with commercials for Delmarva businesses, makes me feel like I'm at the beach house in Delaware, making avocado and tomato sandwiches for lunch as we wait for low tide

OK. Back to work, if one can in fact call compulsively checking email "work".

entry.title: grapheme-color synesthesia; or, the word "irritating" is orange!
entry.content: This morning we discovered in a most comical way that my eight year old is a grapheme-color synesthete, meaning that, in her mind, each letter of the alphabet has a color associated with it. Words have colors, too, usually based on their first letter but not always. Watch the video for an explanation of our discovery, and a demonstration. I am amazed that I have been her mother for eight years and never knew this about her, and she is amazed that not everyone "feels" colors in letters and words like she does. She would like to know if there are other girl synesthetes out there with whom she may correspond, so we're putting out a call to other color-feelers. What color is your "A"?

entry.title: our days
entry.content: School in the mornings, play in the afternoons, writing at night after everyone else has fallen asleep, so the days pass here. Our days have found a new rhythm that quite resembles the old...well, except for the sun. The new kitchen and dining room get lots and lots of delicious morning sun. I love nothing more than to sit in it and bake my poor bones, made frigid by five years spent in one of the snowiest, cloudiest, rainiest cities in the States. I'm not sure when I'll ever be warm again. We're heading to the National Zoo on the Metro today to see Cyrano the giant anteater. I wonder if he likes sitting in the sun, too?

entry.title: the red piano
entry.content:
One day, when I was about five years old, my mama found this piano on the side of the road. She recruited friends to haul it back to our falling-down farmhouse, and then just like that, we had a piano. She played it, I played it a little, and when he visited, my crazy Swedish grandfather played it a lot.

We were very poor when she found that piano. My father still slept on our downstairs couch because he had nowhere to go after the divorce. We scrounged clothing for free from the Rescue Mission. I had sores in the corners of my mouth from malnutrition. You know, that kind of poor.

My nutty mom, my sardonic dad with the Brooklyn accent, my grandparents, friends from church, they would all gather in our kitchen with their guitars and pipes and jokes, and they would talk and smoke and play games of penny poker and laugh and laugh. I would lie on my back under the glass table, between pairs of feet, and watch the cards and coins move across the surface above. Later on my grandfather would crank out tunes on the red piano, and we would dance and sing.

OH MY WORD!! I have spent months and months trying to remember the title to that book, 'A Time To Keep' - I am not even kidding you. I have been to Barnes and Noble and pestered the kids section employees describing this beautiful picture book that revolves around the holidays and months of the year and NO ONE could think of the title or author. But it was always nagging in the back of my child's memory and then, there it is! On your site - in your pictures!!! YAY! Beware, book stores -here I come!
Gorgeous

Thank you for sharing this beautiful post. I am so sorry to learn of your family's loss. I want to thank you for writing about it though...my mother is in Hospice care and will most likely not live through the holidays and I feel like I am mourning every day. Your post really touched me and I appreciate your honesty.
Thank you, ladies. My husband's younger sister and beloved grandmother, both in the same week. I'll pass your hugs on to him, too. :)
I love that star light in the last photo. What a cool place to spend the evening.

Here's the recipe! (I leave out the nuts since my daughter is allergic)
A fixed-bottom, 10-inch tube pan (with 10-cup capacity) is best for this recipe. Note that the streusel is divided into two parts—one for the inner swirls, one for the topping.
Ingredients Streusel 3/4 cup unbleached all-purpose flour (3 3/4 ounces) 3/4 cup granulated sugar (5 1/4 ounces) 1/2 cup packed dark brown sugar (3 1/2 ounces) 2 tablespoons ground cinnamon 2 tablespoons unsalted butter , cold, cut into 2 pieces 1 cup pecans , chopped Cake 12 tablespoons unsalted butter (1 1/2 sticks), softened but still cool, cut into 1/2-inch cubes, plus 2 tablespoons softened butter for greasing pan 4 large eggs 1 1/2 cups sour cream 1 tablespoon vanilla extract 2 1/4 cups unbleached all-purpose flour (11 1/2 ounces) 1 1/4 cups granulated sugar (8 3/4 ounces) 1 tablespoon baking powder 3/4 teaspoon baking soda 3/4 teaspoon table salt
Instructions
1. For the streusel: In food processor, process flour, granulated sugar, 1/4 cup dark brown sugar, and cinnamon until combined, about 15 seconds. Transfer 1 1/4 cups of flour/sugar mixture to small bowl; stir in remaining 1/4 cup brown sugar and set aside to use for streusel filling. Add butter and pecans to mixture in food processor; pulse until nuts and butter resemble small pebbly pieces, about ten 1-second pulses. Set aside to use as streusel topping.
2. For the cake: Adjust oven rack to lowest position and heat oven to 350 degrees. Grease 10-inch tube pan with 2 tablespoons softened butter. Whisk eggs, 1 cup sour cream, and vanilla in medium bowl until combined.
3. Combine flour, sugar, baking powder, baking soda, and salt in bowl of standing mixer; mix on low speed for 30 seconds to blend. Add butter and remaining 1/2 cup sour cream; mix on low speed until dry ingredients are moistened and mixture resembles wet sand, with few large butter pieces remaining, about 1 1/2 minutes. Increase to medium speed and beat until batter comes together, about 10 seconds; scrape down sides of bowl with rubber spatula. Lower speed to medium-low and gradually add egg mixture in 3 additions, beating for 20 seconds after each and scraping down sides of bowl. Increase speed to medium-high and beat until batter is light and fluffy, about 1 minute.
4. Using rubber spatula, spread 2 cups batter in bottom of prepared pan, smoothing surface. Sprinkle evenly with 3/4 cup streusel filling (without butter or nuts). Repeat with another 2 cups batter and remaining 3/4 cup streusel filling (without butter or nuts). Spread remaining batter over, then sprinkle with streusel topping (with butter and nuts).
5. Bake until cake feels firm to touch and long toothpick or skewer inserted into center comes out clean (bits of sugar from streusel may cling to tester), 50 to 60 minutes. Cool cake in pan on wire rack 30 minutes. Invert cake onto rimmed baking sheet (cake will be streusel-side down); remove tube pan, place wire rack on top of cake, and reinvert cake streusel-side up. Cool to room temperature, about 2 hours. Cut into wedges and serve. (Cake can be wrapped in foil and stored at room temperature for up to 5 days.)
Beautiful!

entry.title: handmade homeschool notebook covers
entry.content:
"We are cups, constantly and quietly being filled. The trick is, knowing how to tip ourselves over and let the beautiful stuff out."
Ray Bradbury, on writing

Writing is one of the most important components of our homeschool. I want my children to enjoy their journaling, narrations and compositions, and in my experience the feel of one's notebooks contributes to the overall appeal, or lack thereof, of the work at hand. The standard, black and white marbled covers of their history, science and language arts notebooks not only threatened to make dull work out of a task that should be meaningful and pleasant, but also threatened to trigger one of my migraines. I covered them up with bits from my fabric stash: hers are covered in a petite Japanese print, his are covered with a bold graphic Ikea print. Their notebooks are now personalized, soft, comforting, ready to receive.

entry.title: sun off of my hubcaps
entry.content: This is a picture of the sun reflecting off of my hubcaps and onto the pavement of the parking lot at Target. I was that weird lady you saw pointing her cell phone at the ground. I'm seeing art in everything lately. I suppose it's Spring in my soul, too.
Had a great time...but I still have to work on my homework!! maybe I will have it done in a year;o)

This is such a great way to personalize the presentation of the student's writing. Even students who use loose sheets of paper to write and then gather them in a three ring or loose notebook can benefit from this idea.
The more the student feels the writing reflects "them" - their interests, tastes, values - the stronger their writing becomes and the more it reflects their own voice.
I have encouraged my student's to use Word Art to develop the front of their three ring notebooks to slide into the slip-cover. I think my daughter in particular will prefer this idea of a soft touch, with her choice of colors. Thanks for the idea.
Know the feeling of being behind! I still have to get so much done before the big night... Could you share the cake recepie? Never heard about a coffe-sour cream cake before..
You wacky Swedes.
Wow. I'm so sorry for the pain so many of us seem to be in, and I'm humbled that these words might have helped a bit...
